Description: Incremental model improvements not justifying added complexity or cost.
Benefits materializing later than expected.
Ongoing costs for data, infrastructure, monitoring, and talent.
Historical or sampling bias leading to unfair or inaccurate predictions.
Training data inadvertently containing information unavailable at inference time.
Fragmented data sources across teams or systems.
Not enough samples to train robust models.
High cost or difficulty of annotation, especially for supervised learning.
Missing values, noise, inconsistencies, duplicates, or incorrect labels.
Difficulty embedding AI models into existing applications or workflows.
Inference times exceeding system requirements.
Models performing well in notebooks but failing in production.
Models not designed to handle production-scale data or traffic.
Metrics not aligned with real-world impact.
Inability to measure causal impact in live systems.
Strong validation results that do not translate to production performance.
Ignoring long-term effects or feedback loops.
Bias, discrimination, or unintended societal impacts.
Inability to explain or defend model decisions.
Violations of data protection or AI governance regulations.
Model theft, data poisoning, or adversarial attacks.
Differences between development, testing, and production environments.
Unexpected cloud expenses for training or inference.
Insufficient CPU/GPU capacity or memory.
Fragmented MLOps stack with poor integration.
Excessive tuning effort with marginal performance gains.
Using overly complex models when simpler approaches suffice (or vice versa).
Black-box models that are difficult to interpret or justify.
Poor generalization to unseen data.
Inconsistent results due to unstable pipelines or randomization.
No visibility into model accuracy, bias, or failures post-deployment.
Changes in data distribution or user behavior degrading performance over time.
Manual or brittle retraining pipelines.
Difficulty tracking model, data, and feature versions.
User distrust or reluctance to adopt AI-driven decisions.
Poor collaboration between business, data, and engineering teams.
Shortage of experienced data scientists, ML engineers, or domain experts.
No defined accountability for model performance or failures.
Models optimized for technical metrics rather than business outcomes.
Assuming AI can fully replace human judgment in complex or ambiguous domains.
Vague objectives or attempting to “use AI” without a clear business problem.
Expanding requirements after model development begins.
